sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Surgical intervention is the primary treatment modality for most stages of bladder cancer. TURBT serves as both diagnostic and therapeutic for NMIBC and as a staging procedure for MIBC. Radical cystectomy with pelvic lymphadenectomy is the standard surgical treatment for MIBC stages II and IIIA. For BCG-unresponsive NMIBC, cystectomy is a preferred option. For UTUC, nephroureterectomy with bladder cuff is the standard surgical approach. Partial cystectomy is reserved for highly select patients with cT2 disease in favorable locations.

Principles:
- Adequate TURBT with muscle in specimen is essential for accurate staging; muscle may be omitted only for documented low-grade Ta disease [BL-B]
- Visually complete and safe TURBT is an essential part of bladder preservation and is associated with improved outcomes in non-metastatic settings [BL-B]
- Enhanced cystoscopy (blue light and narrow-band imaging) may be helpful in identifying lesions not visible using white light [BL-B]
- Repeat TURBT within 6 weeks recommended for incomplete initial resection, no muscle in specimen for high-grade disease, large/multifocal lesions, or T1 disease [BL-B]
- Bilateral pelvic lymphadenectomy should be performed with radical cystectomy and partial cystectomy [BL-B]
- A more extensive PLND yields more nodes, increases positive node yield, and may be associated with better survival and lower pelvic recurrence [156-160]
- In appropriately selected patients, approaches preserving the uterus, vagina, fallopian tubes, and/or ovaries should be employed when feasible [149,150]
- Cystectomy should be performed within 3 months of diagnosis if no therapy is given [BL-B]
- Consultation/re-review from experienced GU pathologist as required for rare subtype histology confirmation [BL-C]

cards.name: Transurethral Resection of Bladder Tumor (TURBT)
cards.detail:
Approach: Endoscopic transurethral
Indications: All suspected bladder cancers for diagnosis, staging, and treatment of NMIBC; staging procedure for MIBC; therapeutic for select small solitary T2 lesions
Key complications: Bladder perforation (contraindication to immediate intravesical chemotherapy), bleeding, infection, urinary retention
Operative mortality: Minimal

cards.name: Radical Cystectomy/Cystoprostatectomy
cards.detail:
Approach: Open or robotic (laparoscopic); includes prostatectomy in males (removal of prostate, seminal vesicles, proximal vas deferens, proximal urethra) and commonly hysterectomy in females (removal of uterus, ovaries, fallopian tubes, urethra, part of vagina)
Indications: Primary treatment for cT2, cT3, cT4a MIBC; highly select cT4b responding to primary treatment; BCG-unresponsive high-risk NMIBC; residual high-grade cT1 with aggressive features; aggressive histopathologic subtypes; lymphovascular invasion; concomitant CIS
Key complications: Bleeding, infection, urinary diversion complications (ileal conduit or neobladder), incontinence (with orthotopic diversion), sexual dysfunction, lymphocele, ureteral stricture
Operative mortality: 0.75% to 6.4% per meta-analysis of 13,185 patients [238]

cards.name: Partial Cystectomy
cards.detail:
Approach: Open, laparoscopic, or robotic; includes bilateral pelvic lymphadenectomy
Indications: Highly select cT2 disease with solitary lesion in location amenable to segmental resection with adequate margins; no CIS on random biopsies; cancer in bladder diverticulum; select UTUC cases
Key complications: Similar to radical cystectomy but with retained bladder function; risk of local recurrence; may require ureteral reimplantation
Operative mortality: Comparable to radical cystectomy in experienced centers

cards.name: Radical Nephroureterectomy with Cuff of Bladder
cards.detail:
Approach: Open or minimally invasive (laparoscopic/robotic)
Indications: Primary treatment for non-metastatic high-grade upper GU tract tumors
Key complications: Bleeding, infection, urinary leak, loss of kidney function, bladder cuff recurrence
Operative mortality: Varies by institution

cards.name: TUR of Prostate (TURP)
cards.detail:
Approach: Endoscopic transurethral
Indications: Primary treatment for urothelial carcinoma of the prostate with ductal/acini or prostatic urethral pathology
Key complications: Bleeding, infection, incontinence, erectile dysfunction, TUR syndrome
Operative mortality: Minimal

cards.name: Urethrectomy
cards.detail:
Approach: Distal or total; may include inguinal lymph node dissection in selected cases
Indications: Primary treatment for CIS, Ta, T1 primary carcinoma of the urethra; patients with prior radical cystectomy and cutaneous diversion; T2 disease in selected cases
Key complications: Bleeding, infection, incontinence, urethral stricture, sexual dysfunction
Operative mortality: Low

cards.name: Pelvic Exenteration (category 2B)
cards.detail: Approach: Open; en bloc removal of urethra, bladder, and anterior vagina (female) or prostate (male); may in
